nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still lowland waters, including rice paddies, floodplains, swamps, shallow streams, and irrigation canals; typically found over soft muddy or sandy substrate with abundant leaf litter and aquatic vegetation.

decorPreferences: soft sand substrate (fine, smooth — essential for burrowing and abrasion-free movement)
dense planting or leaf litter to provide daytime hiding cover
PVC tubes, bamboo canes, or slate caves as refuge
driftwood and roots for additional cover
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce light intensity

diet.notes: In the wild, the Lesser Spiny Eel feeds primarily on small worms, insect larvae, crustaceans, and other bottom-dwelling invertebrates. In the aquarium it generally accepts frozen or thawed bloodworms, tubifex, brine shrimp, daphnia, and small pieces of earthworm. With patience, some individuals can be trained to accept sinking carnivore pellets and wafers over time. Because the species is nocturnal, feeding after lights-out is recommended — it reduces competition from tankmates and gives the eel a better chance to locate food. Live foods can be especially useful for newly acquired specimens or individuals that are slow to settle in and begin feeding.

goodWith: medium peaceful community fish (tetras, rasboras, danios)
avoidWith: very small fish (under 3 cm) — risk of predation
small shrimp and small invertebrates (will be eaten)
fin-nippers (tiger barbs) — spiny eels' soft body and elongated fins are vulnerable
other spiny eels (territorial skirmishes, especially in smaller tanks)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase specimen photo of Macrognathus siamensis showing characteristic ocelli along dorsal fin base.

summary: The Lesser Spiny Eel (Macrognathus siamensis) is among the most beginner-accessible spiny eels in the hobby, reaching 25–35 cm — smaller than the Tire Track or Fire Eel. A distinctive row of eye-spot ocelli along the dorsal fin base earns it the trade name 'Peacock Eel'. Requires soft sand for burrowing and a tightly fitted lid, as escape attempts are common.
careNotes:
The Lesser Spiny Eel is widely considered one of the most accessible spiny eels for aquarium keeping, remaining manageable at 25–35 cm versus the 50–90 cm reached by the Tire Track Eel (Mastacembelus armatus) or Fire Eel (M. erythrotaenia). Like all members of the family Mastacembelidae, it has a scaleless or near-scaleless body and lacks a functional gill cover, which makes it highly sensitive to copper-based medications and most standard ich treatments. Copper-free alternatives should always be used — heat treatment, salt-based options, or formalin/malachite-green products applied at a reduced dose with close observation are generally recommended.

A soft, fine sand substrate at least 5 cm deep is essential: the fish buries itself completely for security and will rest partially submerged during the day. Coarse gravel causes abrasion to the delicate skin and should be avoided. A tightly fitted lid with no gaps is mandatory — the Lesser Spiny Eel is a confirmed escape artist capable of squeezing through surprisingly small openings.

The species is strictly nocturnal, and daytime feeding attempts are usually ignored. Target feeding with sinking foods such as bloodworms, tubifex, or earthworm pieces after lights-out gives the fish a chance to eat without competition. Multiple ocelli — black eye-spots ringed with yellow or cream — are arranged along the base of the dorsal fin, and these spots are the primary visual feature distinguishing M. siamensis from the similar Macrognathus aculeatus (sometimes called the True Peacock Eel), which tends to have fewer, more irregularly placed spots and a more elongated snout.

The Lesser Spiny Eel is less predatory than larger mastacembelids and generally coexists peacefully with medium-sized community fish, though very small fish under 3 cm and ornamental shrimp are at risk of being eaten. Conspecific aggression tends to be low in larger tanks with multiple refuges; in smaller tanks, competition for burrow sites can cause stress. Water quality must remain stable and clean — spiny eels are prone to bacterial skin infections and septicaemia when conditions deteriorate.
breedingNotes: The Lesser Spiny Eel is rarely bred in home aquariums. In the wild, spawning is associated with seasonal flooding and rising water levels. Aquarium breeding has been reported occasionally in large, well-planted tanks with soft water, where rainy-season conditions were simulated through water changes using slightly cooler, softer water along with abundant planting for cover. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants and no parental care is provided. Fry are very small and require infusoria or micro worms as first foods. Commercial aquaculture occurs in parts of Southeast Asia, so most specimens available in the trade are wild-caught or farm-raised rather than hobbyist-bred.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Macrognathus siamensis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 35 cm), temperature range, pH, hardness, native range (Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam), habitat (slow-moving/still lowland waters, rice paddies), diet (invertivores — worms, crustaceans, insect larvae), care notes (soft sand, nocturnal, copper sensitivity, escape risk), and taxonomic confirmation (Mastacembelidae, Synbranchiformes). Species identity note: SeriouslyFish confirms M. siamensis is the commonly traded 'Peacock Eel', distinct from M. aculeatus.

additional.name: FishBase — Macrognathus siamensis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max reported 35 cm TL), native range (Mekong basin: Thailand, Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam; also Malay Peninsula), taxonomy (Mastacembelidae, Synbranchiformes), habitat (benthopelagic, freshwater, 22–28°C), and diet (invertivore). Confirmed common name 'Peacock Eel' listed in synonymy alongside 'Siamese Spiny Eel'.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Spiny Eel Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade/hobby care perspective. Confirmed soft sand substrate requirement, high escape risk, nocturnal feeding, copper sensitivity (scaleless classification), and recommendation as most beginner-friendly spiny eel in the genus. Tank size guidance (55+ gallon / ~210 L for larger spiny eels; lesser spiny eel manageable at 40 gal / 150 L). Noted species confusion between M. siamensis and M. aculeatus in the trade.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Spiny Eels species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed genus-level care notes for Macrognathus: soft substrate essential, copperSensitive due to scaleless/thin-skinned body, strict nocturnal activity pattern, high escape risk, preference for carnivorous diet (worms, larvae). Noted ocelli pattern on dorsal fin as key identification feature for siamensis in the trade. Verified temperature range 24–28°C and pH 6.5–7.5 consistent with SeriouslyFish data.

additional.name: FishBase — Macrognathus aculeatus (comparison)
additional.notes: Consulted for species-confusion disambiguation. M. aculeatus (True Peacock Eel) reaches similar length but has a broader native range (South and Southeast Asia including India and Sri Lanka) and fewer, more irregular dorsal ocelli. M. siamensis has a distinct row of clearly defined, consistently spaced eye-spots bordered with yellow/cream. Taxonomic note: both species share the common name 'Peacock Eel' in trade, but siamensis is confirmed as the more commonly exported species from Thailand/Vietnam farms.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquariums; most trade specimens are wild-caught or aquaculture-farmed. Seasonal conditioning with cooler, softer water changes may trigger spawning in large planted tanks. Eggs are scattered among vegetation and no parental care is provided.
